"What is a man if not a vessel of agony wrapped in dreams?" — A life lived through observation, language, and the quiet insistence of thought.

Scroll
01 — Who I Am

Not achievements.
But questions.

Hashmatullah Reyaz Malik  ·  Kashmir

My name is Hashmatullah Reyaz Malik. I come from Kashmir — a place that teaches you early how to carry something without showing it. I was born into a body that moves differently through the world: I live with Osteogenesis Imperfecta Type III, and I use a wheelchair. It has not defined me — but it has deepened me.

I do not write from comfort. I write from the edges of understanding — from the place between what I feel and what I can explain. Most of what I know, I learned not from certainty but from watching things fall apart and studying the shape of the wreckage.

There is something I have always believed: that a person who pays attention to life — truly pays attention — will find more wisdom in a single Tuesday than a textbook offers in a year. I have tried to be that person. I am still trying.

I write sometimes. I take photographs sometimes. Both are the same gesture — a hand reaching out before the moment disappears. Neither is meant to be impressive. Both are meant to be true.

I do not have a neat story to offer. What I have is a handful of fragments, a few honest questions, and the quiet belief that being alive means you owe the world your full attention — even when it exhausts you.

Join me on the journey of exploring the world, if you are trying to understand it the way I do.

Fragments & Thoughts

"How could you live and have no story to tell?"
"What is a man if not a vessel of agony wrapped in dreams?"
"We are all crucified between the poles of what we are and what we might have been."
"I have walked through the catacombs of my soul and found only echoes. Yet even an echo is a voice trying to return."
"I buried my anguish like a coward, thinking that silence was strength. But silence is a tomb, and I was its inhabitant."
"The heart is a prison where God and the devil play chess, and I, fool that I am, watch — believing the pieces are free."
"If you do not tell your story, it will be told for you."
"I loved. I died."

"Join me on the journey of exploring the world, if you are trying to understand it the way I do."— Hashmatullah Reyaz Malik

Literary Essay  /  Existential Prose

Limerent Misery

by Hashmatullah Reyaz Malik

Introduction — Why Do We Suffer?

Why do you think we suffer? Look around you — there are people who are always suffering, and you keep hoping that their suffering will end. You might paint a picture in your mind that this will pass soon — but for them, it never does.

Life keeps giving them shocks, like earthquakes striking at intervals. For a moment, there is happiness, and then suddenly, everything falls quiet again.

I knew a person, and I thought the same — that this was only for a brief moment, that he would suffer for a while, and eventually the universe would reward him with something he had never imagined. I always believed that life has its ups and downs, and that, in the end, you will find stability.

You begin to notice patterns you once ignored. The same people, the same kind of suffering, repeating without any clear reason. And you start to wonder — is life really balanced the way we believe it to be?

And once you begin to see that, the idea of "everything will eventually be okay" no longer feels like truth. It starts to feel like something we tell ourselves — not because it is certain, but because we need it to be.

And then, this person I used to know died. Just a few days before his death, I met him. We spoke about life. I asked how things were going, and in a weary voice, he said only a few words — nothing is going well.

My belief lost its meaning. No matter how I think about him now — it changes nothing. Because he is no longer there to see any of it. His life has dissolved into what cannot be returned.

I will not go into the details of who he was, but I must say this — he was a good man. He deserved change. He deserved to be happy. He deserved peace. But it did not happen that way.

The Chaos Within

Inside each of us, there is often a hidden battle that others do not see. We struggle because the things we really want are always out of reach, while others seem to get them without trying.

Just like we can see a mighty star shining brightly in the sky but cannot actually reach it, there are some things that will always be beyond our grasp.

I Am Misery — Page 156

I am the heat of summers you hate, not the warmth you wait for in winters. I am the heavy rain you open the umbrella for, not the tiny droplets you are ready to soak in. I am the flower for graves — symbol of regret — not the one in bouquets, symbol of love.

I am the other side of the story. The side character you would not notice. The one you will never know — even if I stood in front of you. I am misery.

Rebellion — Page 223

And I lived my life waiting for everyone to love me. To become the shining star. The perfect child. Always gentle, always kind. Woke up in a cage of your expectations and woke up in a cage of my own death.

I am breaking out tonight. Making my own mistakes. Finding my own way. Not your star. Not your diamond. Not your example. Not your puppet. Now it is rebellion.

On Love & Absence

Love. Because misery is not only shaped by what happens to us — it is also shaped by what is missing. And one of the deepest absences a person can feel is the absence of being loved.

Perhaps that is what every human being is searching for — not perfection, not even happiness, but to feel wanted. And when that is missing, even a stable life can begin to feel empty. Man is built to survive — but not alone. The least we owe to those who live this reality is to not turn away from it.

Hashmatullah Reyaz Malik Kashmir  ·  A Universe of One  ·
